games.explanation: Ben Brown is being priced like a competent right-hander, which conveniently ignores that his entire profile collapses against bats that don't share his handedness. Toronto runs out a lineup that punishes exactly that flaw, with Guerrero in particular doing his loudest work against righties and Springer right behind him posting a .415 xwOBA in the same split. Gausman on the other side is the calmer, more predictable arm in this matchup by a wide margin, and a park that suppresses runs only helps the pitcher you trust more. Getting plus money on the better team, the better starter, and the better matchup is the kind of stack the market doesn't usually hand out. +105 is a gift you cash.

Nobody is going to argue Skubal is the worse pitcher tonight, but +194 is the price of a team that has no chance, and the White Sox actually have one. Skubal has been a buzzsaw against lefties and merely very good against righties, where his xwOBA-against jumps closer to league-average ace territory, and Chicago happens to run out a stack of right-handed bats with real damage in their swings (Grichuk, Montgomery, Vargas all qualify). Fedde is the problem, obviously, but the sim has this as a one-run game because Detroit's lineup outside of Greene is shallower than the reputation suggests, and a breezy night in a park that plays up should help the wrong team often enough. At nearly 2-to-1, you only need it to happen four times out of ten, and the math says it happens closer to six.

Jax is a strikeout machine against righties and a different pitcher entirely once you flip the box, and Washington happens to roll out a lineup tilted exactly the wrong way for him. Wood, García, and Abrams are all left-handed bats, and the splits on Jax versus lefties balloon to a .314 xwOBA with the whiff rate dropping and the walks creeping. That is the kind of starter profile the market keeps pricing as elite because the overall line looks shiny, even when the actual matchup undoes most of the edge. Cavalli is no ace, but he keeps the barrel rate down and that is usually enough to hang around against a Tampa lineup that leans heavily on Aranda and Díaz to do the damage. At +110 on a dog the math has winning outright more than half the time, the price is the whole argument.

Backing a dog behind Randy Vasquez against Jacob deGrom looks insane on paper, which is exactly why the number drifted to a place it shouldn't be. deGrom has been everything advertised, but the Padres roll out three right-handed bats (Tatis, Machado, Merrill) who all post xwOBAs north of .350 against righties, and deGrom's contact-quality numbers to right-handers are quietly more vulnerable than the strikeout rate suggests. Texas, meanwhile, runs a top of the order that's been league-average at best, which softens the Vasquez problem more than the market wants to admit. The sim has this as a one-run game; the board has it as a mismatch. At +136, you only need San Diego to be alive, and they are.

Arizona is a tick better on paper, but a one-run sim projection does not justify pricing Minnesota like an underdog you can ignore. Buxton has been the kind of right-handed bat that punishes mistakes from anyone, and Larnach's .335 xwOBA against righties keeps the lineup honest deeper than the Diamondbacks' card suggests. The closed roof neutralizes the park's home run suppression just enough to matter, and a near coin-flip game does not belong at this number. +145 on the better-than-advertised side is the entire pitch.

The market is pricing this like the Yankees are supposed to roll, but the simulated game is a one-run coin flip dressed up as a mismatch. Schlittler has been pretty hittable against righties this year, posting a .329 xwOBA-against that doesn't square with the chalk treatment, and Cincinnati's right-handed bats at the top of the order are exactly the profile that's been giving him trouble. Sal Stewart in particular has been barreling everything in sight, and the wind blowing out in a homer-friendly park gives the Reds a cheap path to keeping pace. At +105 on the runner and a projected margin of one-tenth of a run, you're getting plus money on the side the math actually likes.
